title: "I can't get Windows to start :("

### Post by Ruediger on 2005-01-12
After installing Ubuntu 4.10, I can't get into my Windows partition. I've searched a lot and this seeems to be a quite common problem, unfortunately none of the solutions proposed worked for me.

The error message I get is:
```

root (HD0,0)
Filesystem type unknown, partition type 0x7

```

My menu.lst:
```

# Generated by grubconf-0.5.1
default=0
timeout=10

title Windows XP
#:1 <-- type: 0 => linux, 1 => windows, 2 => other
	rootnoverify (hd0,0)
	makeactive
	chainloader +1
title Ubuntu, kernel 2.6.8.1-3-386 
#:2 <-- type: 0 => linux, 1 => windows, 2 => other
root		(hd0,4)
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.8.1-3-386 root=/dev/hda5 ro quiet splash
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.8.1-3-386
savedefault
boot
title Ubuntu, kernel 2.6.8.1-3-386 (recovery mode)
#:2 <-- type: 0 => linux, 1 => windows, 2 => other
root		(hd0,4)
kernel		/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.8.1-3-386 root=/dev/hda5 ro single
initrd		/boot/initrd.img-2.6.8.1-3-386
savedefault
boot
title Memory test
#:2 <-- type: 0 => linux, 1 => windows, 2 => other
root		(hd0,4)
kernel		/boot/memtest86+.bin

```

When I run parted, I get the following warn:
```

Warning: Unable to align partition properly.  This probably means that another
partitioning tool generated an incorrect partition table, because it 
didn't have the correct BIOS geometry.  It is safe to ignore, but 
ignoring may cause (fixable) problems with some boot loaders.

```

The output of parted>print:
```

Disk geometry for /dev/hda: 0.000-38172.750 megabytes
Disk label type: msdos
Minor    Start       End     Type      Filesystem  Flags
1          0.031  33039.931  primary   ntfs        boot
2      33039.962  38170.063  extended              lba
5      33039.962  37142.468  logical   ext3
6      37142.499  38170.063  logical   linux-swap

```

I've already tried to change my BIOS setting from auto to LBA, but this will give me error 18 in GRUB.

---

### Post by Ruediger on 2005-01-13
Maybe reinstalling GRUB in a new partition (/boot) will solve this?

Can anyone explain me if this is possible, and how I should do it?

---

### Post by wallijonn on 2005-01-13
If your Ubuntu boots, you may first want to create a GRUB boot floppy. (Search HOWTO section).

Then you would boot from the WXP CD and f8, safe mode, write a new MBR, reboot.

You would then use the floppy to boot into Ubuntu and eject the floppy to boot into WXP.

---

### Post by Ruediger on 2005-01-13
[QUOTE=wallijonn]If your Ubuntu boots, you may first want to create a GRUB boot floppy. (Search HOWTO section).

Then you would boot from the WXP CD and f8, safe mode, write a new MBR, reboot.

You would then use the floppy to boot into Ubuntu and eject the floppy to boot into WXP.[/QUOTE]

Thanks for helping but I finally found the solution.

I found this page: [http://portal.suse.com/sdb/en/2004/05/fhassel_windows_not_booting91.html](http://portal.suse.com/sdb/en/2004/05/fhassel_windows_not_booting91.html) and since I have a SUSE 9.1 DVD I followed the steps on the floppy section. It worked perfectly. :)

---

### Post by wallijonn on 2005-01-13
Good find, good fix. <Thumbs up>.

### Post by kharl on 2006-11-08
> **kharl said:**
> I think I have the same problem now after Ubuntu 6.10 installation

In my case there was another main problem described as "win xp freezes at mup.sys". 

[http://www.hardwareanalysis.com/content/topic/31874/?o=0](http://www.hardwareanalysis.com/content/topic/31874/?o=0) 

Solution: set ACPI to Enabled in BIOS... 

Of course I am still getting warnings from sfdisk or testdisk about CHS/LBA mismatch in partition. Other tools report no problems at all. Dual boot is working fine.

---
